claims.title: get_project_root() resolves to the scan target path
claims.detail: get_project_root() (paths.py:13-18) returns the project root — Path(os.environ.get('DESLOPPIFY_ROOT', Path.cwd())).resolve() or a runtime-context override. The scan target path is get_project_root() / 'src' (see get_default_path(), get_src_path()). The project root is the parent of the scan target, not the scan target itself. The spirit of the claim (plugins are loaded from the directory being analyzed) is correct, but the precise framing is wrong.

claims.title: Plugin auto-loading from .desloppify/plugins/*.py executes arbitrary Python files without user consent
claims.detail: discovery.py:95-113 constructs get_project_root() / '.desloppify' / 'plugins', iterates all *.py files, and executes each via spec.loader.exec_module(mod) — direct in-process Python execution. No warning, no opt-in prompt, no user consent. Failures are caught silently at DEBUG level. The code is explicitly commented '# Discover user plugins from <active-project-root>/.desloppify/plugins/*.py', confirming this is intentional.

claims.title: This constitutes poor engineering
claims.detail: The plugin loading is an intentional, explicitly documented feature. It follows standard dev tool conventions: pytest executes conftest.py, ESLint loads .eslintrc.js, pre-commit loads hook scripts from project root. Python provides no meaningful sandboxing regardless of approach. The potential risk (untrusted repos containing malicious .desloppify/plugins/*.py) is a use-case consideration, not a code quality defect. There is no scoring impact.

notes: The observation that plugins are executed without consent is technically accurate, and the code refs are correct (discovery.py:95-113, paths.py:13-18). However, the 'get_project_root() = scan target path' framing is imprecise (project root != scan target), and the 'poor engineering' claim does not hold — this is an intentional plugin extension point following standard dev tool patterns. No fix warranted. Verdict: NO.
